# Questions in Livestream 18

Live Stream #18: For the Good Life

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/N4pgeufCqLQ?t=296" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:56</a> | If some Israelites were literate in Egyptian, could the original Torah have been written in Egyptian before being copied into Paleo-Hebrew? | He said that was unlikely. The Torah does contain Egyptian loanwords, but its syntax does not look like a work first composed in Egyptian. | Falk said Egyptian and Hebrew have very different syntax, and translation from Egyptian would normally leave recognizable patterns in the target language. He allowed that the Torah uses many Egyptian loanwords, but he thought its mode of expression points to original composition in a Semitic language rather than Egyptian. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/N4pgeufCqLQ?t=458" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">7:38</a> | Besides Papyrus Amherst 63, are there other texts that combine Jewish or Hebrew patterns with Egyptian script? | He said Papyrus Amherst 63 is very unusual and he did not know of many real parallels. | He described Papyrus Amherst 63 as an exceptional Demotic text, noting that Demotic is a difficult specialty and not something he personally reads. He connected the papyrus to Judean or Aramaic psalms and Elephantine mercenaries, but said examples of Demotic being used this way are not common. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/N4pgeufCqLQ?t=740" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:20</a> | What does he think of the reported space-rock destruction at Tell el-Hammam, and does it fit Sodom and Gomorrah at all? | He was cautious. He had concerns about the article's methodology and wanted more response from specialists before accepting the skyburst claim or the Sodom identification. | Falk said the proposed date, mineralogical claims, and unpublished stratigraphy all raised questions for him. Even if a skyburst eventually proved plausible, he wanted proper field reports and evidence before accepting either the destruction model or the identification with Sodom. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/N4pgeufCqLQ?t=1091" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">18:11</a> | Does he think more royal tombs will be found in the Valley of the Kings, and which New Kingdom kings' tombs or mummies are still missing? | Probably yes in a broad sense. He doubted many more kingly tombs remain, but thought more royal burials may still be there; he named Amenhotep I, either Akhenaten or Smenkhkare, and Ramesses VIII as major missing cases. | He distinguished between tombs of kings and other royal burials, saying queens' and princes' tombs may still be overlooked, as KV5 once was. For missing New Kingdom kings, he emphasized Amenhotep I, one unresolved Amarna king depending on the interpretation of KV55, and Ramesses VIII, whose burial may lie outside the Valley of the Kings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/N4pgeufCqLQ?t=1433" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:53</a> | How likely is it that the Tanakh was ever translated into Egyptian in antiquity, given Jewish temples in places like Elephantine and Leontopolis? | He thought that was unlikely in earlier Egyptian, though he noted that Old Testament material was later translated into Coptic, the last phase of Egyptian. | He said Jewish communities in Egypt show cultural contact and some intertextual influence in both directions, but he did not think the Tanakh was translated as a whole into earlier Egyptian. He contrasted that with later Coptic evidence, where substantial Old Testament material is attested in the final phase of the Egyptian language. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/N4pgeufCqLQ?t=1610" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">26:50</a> | If the Avaris Israelites were literate in Egyptian, would the Ten Commandments written by the finger of the Lord originally have been in Egyptian? | He said no. Avaris was multilingual, but after leaving Egypt the Israelites would mostly have used a West Semitic dialect, so he expected the commandments in early Hebrew rather than Egyptian. | Falk said Avaris was multilingual, with people using both Egyptian and a West Semitic dialect. Once the Israelites left Egypt, he expected Egyptian to be used only rarely and thought the commandments would have been in the Semitic language that became Hebrew. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/N4pgeufCqLQ?t=1721" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:41</a> | What was the extent of Egyptian cultural and linguistic influence on Judah during the 26th dynasty, especially under Necho II and Apries? | He said Necho II is the clearer case because the historical evidence is better, but the subject remains patchy and not especially well covered. | Falk said Apries is harder to discuss because the evidence is sparse, while Necho II appears more often in Assyrian and Babylonian material. He argued that 26th dynasty contact with Judah was much more limited than 18th dynasty imperial control, consisting mainly of occasional military encounters rather than Egyptian garrisons or administrative centers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/N4pgeufCqLQ?t=1987" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:07</a> | Did Jesus's family's time in Egypt leave any clear Egyptian influence on Jesus? | He said no clear Egyptian influence can be identified. Jewish communities in Roman Egypt were culturally distinct enough that this would not be expected. | He said first-century Egypt had strong Jewish communities in places such as Alexandria, Elephantine, Leontopolis, and Pelusium, so a fleeing Jewish family could blend into an existing Jewish enclave. Because the family probably did not stay long and did not need deep interaction with native Egyptian culture, he saw no clear Egyptian influence on Jesus. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/N4pgeufCqLQ?t=2185" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">36:25</a> | Speaking of tombs, what is his favorite tomb in the Valley of the Kings? | He favored one of the later Ramesside tombs and especially praised the beauty of the dynasty 20 royal tombs. | After checking his memory, Falk identified KV9, the tomb of Ramesses VI, as his favorite. He praised its decorated corridor, preserved color, and black-and-yellow vaulted ceiling, calling it the best tomb in the Valley and noting that it requires a special ticket. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/N4pgeufCqLQ?t=2369" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">39:29</a> | Should elim and bene elohim in Psalm 29:1 and Job 1:6 be translated as sons of God or sons of gods? | He said Psalm 29:1 is best understood as "sons of gods," while Job 1:6 uses a different construction and should be read as "sons of God." | Falk looked up the Hebrew during the stream and treated the Psalm 29 phrase as a plural construction meaning "sons of gods," without an English definite article. He then contrasted Job 1:6 as a different construction, where he read the phrase as "sons of God." |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/N4pgeufCqLQ?t=2700" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:00</a> | What is the best book on KV5, and what about the popular-level book on it? | He said he had not seen the preliminary report, but pointed to major Theban Mapping Project work as the more dependable direction. | He could not evaluate the KV5 preliminary report because he had not seen it. Instead, he pointed to the Theban Mapping Project atlas of the Valley of the Kings as the kind of work worth waiting for, praising Kent Weeks as an excavator, mapmaker, and speaker. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/N4pgeufCqLQ?t=2859" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:39</a> | Is KV5 the site Kent Weeks has been working on for nearly thirty years? | Yes. Falk said Weeks excavated KV5, recognized it was more than a small foyer, and also led major Theban Mapping Project work. | Falk confirmed that KV5 is Weeks's site and said Weeks was the one who recognized it as far more than a small foyer for a noble tomb. He also tied Weeks to the Theban Mapping Project and the atlas of the Theban Acropolis. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/N4pgeufCqLQ?t=2920" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:40</a> | What was the late relationship between Egypt and Judah under Zedekiah and Hophra, politically, economically, and socially? | He said the relationship centered on Judah's attempted alliance with Egypt against Babylon, but the details are difficult to pin down. | Falk said Zedekiah tried to establish an Egyptian alliance in case Babylon returned, and Hophra became an unreliable ally. He noted broader Egyptian-Judahite contact through trade goods, iconography, military campaigns, and Egypt's outsized role in Israel's history, but said the depth of late ties remains uncertain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/N4pgeufCqLQ?t=3164" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:44</a> | Are there Babylonian exilic loanwords in the Torah? | He said no in any strong sense. Similarities usually reflect shared Semitic roots rather than clear borrowing from the exile. | He explained that Babylonian and Hebrew are both Semitic, so similar words often reflect common ancestry rather than borrowing. He said the Torah does not show vocabulary specific to the Neo-Babylonian period and that non-Semitic loanwords, such as Persian terms in later books, make stronger dating evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/N4pgeufCqLQ?t=3355" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">55:55</a> | What does he think of attempts to connect Tell el-Hammam's destruction with both Sodom and Jericho's collapsed wall? | He rejected that connection. Jericho's relevant destruction horizon is far too early to match the proposed Tell el-Hammam event. | Falk said the chronology makes the proposed connection untenable. Whether Tell el-Hammam is dated around 1668 BC or more conventionally around 1700 BC, he said that is far too early for Jericho's wall destruction, especially if one is discussing an Exodus-related 13th-century horizon. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/N4pgeufCqLQ?t=3512" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">58:32</a> | Do the Elephantine Jews prove pre-exilic polytheism, including belief that Yahweh had a wife? | He said Elephantine does show Jewish polytheistic practice, but that is not surprising because the Bible itself already describes widespread pre-exilic polytheism in Israel and Judah. | Falk said Elephantine texts suggest Yahweh was paired with Anat and that the community practiced offerings resembling nearby Egyptian religious practice. He emphasized that this does not surprise him, because biblical prophets repeatedly condemn Israelite idolatry and real fidelity to monotheism becomes much clearer only after the Babylonian exile. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/N4pgeufCqLQ?t=3872" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:04:32</a> | Is the Abraham-Hammurabi synchronism contested? | Yes, heavily. He said both skeptical scholars and conservative scholars contest it, though for different reasons. | He said skeptical scholars often contest the synchronism because they treat Abraham as mythical, while some conservative scholars dislike an 18th-century BC Abraham and prefer an earlier date. He also mentioned linguistic objections to the Hammurabi-Amraphel connection but argued that sound shifts across languages make the etymology possible and that the larger body of synchronisms forms a strong inductive case. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/N4pgeufCqLQ?t=4079" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:07:59</a> | Does the Middle Kingdom get short shrift? | He said not really. It seems less glamorous and has fewer surviving monuments, but it remains important and well worth study. | Falk said the Middle Kingdom attracts fewer books partly because its surviving material leans heavily toward texts, while temples and decorated tombs are much less available. He acknowledged that textual specialists can do significant work on the period, but said its lost monuments make it less visually attractive than the New Kingdom. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/N4pgeufCqLQ?t=4297" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:11:37</a> | Are there Old Persian loanwords in the Torah? | He said no. He said Persian loanwords appear in later works such as Ezra, Nehemiah, and perhaps Daniel, but not in the Torah. | Falk stated directly that there are no Persian loanwords in the Torah. He contrasted that with later biblical books, where Persian terms do appear, and added that the Torah may contain a Hittite loanword but not Persian vocabulary. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/N4pgeufCqLQ?t=4357" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:37</a> | Why did Moses have to remove his sandals at the burning bush? | Because he was entering holy ground. Falk connected it with sacred-space rules, including priestly barefoot practice in both Israel and Egypt. | Falk said God's appearance created sacred space within an otherwise profane world. He connected Moses' sandals to purity rules, explaining that leather or animal-skin footwear was not fit for sacred space and comparing the practice to Egyptian priests who used papyrus sandals after ritual bathing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/N4pgeufCqLQ?t=4533" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:15:33</a> | Does he have an opinion on the book of Daniel, or recommended reading on it? | Yes. He recommended a conservative volume by Wiseman, Fairman, and Kitchen as a strong treatment of Daniel's dating and historical issues. | Falk recommended a small out-of-print volume on Daniel by Wiseman, Fairman, and Kitchen. He described it as a strong, conservative, "biblical maximalist" treatment by accomplished scholars that directly addresses Daniel's dating issues. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/N4pgeufCqLQ?t=4616" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:56</a> | Why did Babylonians fear lunar eclipses? | Because eclipses were abnormal, unpredictable disruptions of the expected heavenly cycles and so were taken as bad omens. | He said Babylonians lived by lunar and solar cycles, watched the sky closely, and used observational calendars for matters such as temple festivals. Because eclipses interrupted the expected regularity of the heavens, they were naturally interpreted as bad omens, much like other unusual sky events. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/N4pgeufCqLQ?t=4820" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:20</a> | Are there any documentaries on Egypt or other ancient kingdoms on Netflix or other streaming services that he would recommend for teenagers? | He could not recommend Netflix titles because he does not watch it, but he did mention a few Egypt documentaries he thought were good. | Falk said he does not own a television or watch Netflix, so he could not recommend titles from that platform. He did recommend a documentary or docudrama about the discovery of Tutankhamun's tomb, especially for its portrayal of the personalities involved in the discovery. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/N4pgeufCqLQ?t=4971" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:51</a> | Does "the stone the builders rejected has become the head cornerstone" only make sense with a pyramid, and were pyramids known throughout the ancient Near East? | He said no. Pyramids were rare outside Egypt and Nubia, ziggurats are not the same thing, and cornerstones can belong to temples and other building projects too. | Falk said pyramids were not common across the ancient Near East; Nubia built many small pyramids, Egypt stopped major pyramid building after Ahmose I, and Mesopotamian ziggurats were raised temple platforms rather than pyramids. He added that cornerstones also appear in temples and other construction, so the phrase does not require a pyramid setting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/N4pgeufCqLQ?t=5203" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:26:43</a> | After Israel first reached the promised land and then wandered forty years, does the Bible imply they went back into Sinai? | He said the text does seem to place them back in the Sinai region, especially around Kadesh-barnea. | He said the biblical itinerary appears to keep the Israelites around Kadesh-barnea and the Sinai region rather than sending them far into Midian. He also noted there were enough local resources, including some water, for survival in that area. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/N4pgeufCqLQ?t=5321" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:28:41</a> | Why did the Jews have to wear phylacteries during the Shema? | He treated phylacteries as a concrete ritual reminder of covenant obligations, not as something connected to the lunar-eclipse discussion that prompted the follow-up. | Falk was unsure that phylacteries specifically had to be worn during the Shema, but tied the practice to commandments about placing God's word on the hands and forehead. He said phylacteries, an Egyptian loanword and a practice with Egyptian parallels, reminded Israelites that God's word should govern both thought and daily work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/N4pgeufCqLQ?t=5582" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:33:02</a> | Where does the idea of Samaritan gods with dog heads and human bodies in 2 Kings 17:31 come from? | He said he did not know. After the verse was supplied, he said he did not see that detail in the text and was not familiar enough with the Avites, Nibhaz, or Tartak to explain the origin. | Falk first asked for the biblical reference and said he did not know the tradition offhand. After looking at 2 Kings 17:31, he said he did not see the dog-headed description in the verse and was not sufficiently familiar with the Avites or their deities Nibhaz and Tartak to explain the claim. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/N4pgeufCqLQ?t=5791" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:36:31</a> | What is written on the pyramids? | Very little is written on real pyramids. He said Pyramid Texts begin inside pyramids with Unis, later developing into Coffin Texts and the Book of the Dead. | Falk said real pyramids generally have almost no inscriptions. Beginning with Unis in Dynasty 6, Pyramid Texts were written inside pyramids; he described them as magical spells for preserving the dead in the afterlife and as the tradition that later develops into the Coffin Texts and the Book of the Dead. |
